title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
LOCAL_GET_CONTIG_INFO (upstream helper)
Source: modules/local/get_contig_info/main.nf
Parses ##contig header lines from the VCF to build a name-to-numeric mapping (e.g. I → 1, II → 2, MtDNA → 7). This mapping is required because PLINK and GCTA expect numeric chromosome identifiers.
Commands:
# Extract ##contig headers and build tab-separated mapping (name → number)
zcat input.vcf.gz | head -n 200 | grep "##contig" | \
    awk 'BEGIN{COUNT=1}{split($1,A,"="); split(A[3],B,","); CHROM=B[1];
         printf "%s\t%i\n", CHROM, COUNT; COUNT=COUNT+1}' > contig_mapping.tsv

# Extract just the chromosome names
cut -f 1 contig_mapping.tsv > contigs.txt
Outputs:







File
Description




contig_mapping.tsv
Tab-separated file: chromosome name (col 1) and numeric ID (col 2)


contigs.txt
One chromosome name per line (used by main.nf for mito filtering and chromosome list)



Used by:

BCFTOOLS_RENAME_CHROMS — consumes contig_mapping.tsv as the rename mapping file
main.nf workflow logic — reads contigs.txt to identify the mitochondrial chromosome name and generate the list of autosomal chromosomes

title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
1. BCFTOOLS_EXTRACT_STRAINS
Source: modules/bcftools/extract_strains/main.nf
Subsets the VCF to only the strains in the current strain set and removes any sites with missing genotypes. This produces a clean, strain-specific VCF for downstream processing.
Commands:
# Subset samples and filter missing sites, then index
bcftools view --threads ${task.cpus} -s ${strains} -O u ${vcf} | \
bcftools filter -i N_MISSING=0 -O z -o ${group}.vcf.gz
bcftools index --tbi ${group}.vcf.gz

Purpose
Docs




bcftools view -s
Subset to specified sample names
bcftools view


bcftools filter -i N_MISSING=0
Keep only sites where no sample has a missing genotype
bcftools filter


bcftools index --tbi
Create a tabix (.tbi) index for the output VCF
bcftools index



Key flags:

-O u — pipe as uncompressed BCF (faster for piping between bcftools commands)
-O z — write bgzf-compressed VCF output

Direction
File
Description

Comma-separated list of strain names

text:
2. BCFTOOLS_RENAME_CHROMS
Source: modules/bcftools/rename_chroms/main.nf
Renames chromosome identifiers from text (I, II, III, …) to numeric (1, 2, 3, …) using the mapping file produced by LOCAL_GET_CONTIG_INFO. Numeric chromosome names are required by PLINK and GCTA.
Commands:
# Stream VCF, rename chromosomes, compress and index
bcftools view --threads ${task.cpus} -O u ${vcf} | \
bcftools annotate --rename-chrs chr_mapping.tsv -O z -o ${id}_renamed.vcf.gz
bcftools index --tbi ${id}_renamed.vcf.gz



Command
Purpose
Docs




bcftools view
Stream VCF as uncompressed BCF for piping
bcftools view


bcftools annotate --rename-chrs
Rename chromosomes using a tab-separated mapping file
bcftools annotate


bcftools index --tbi
Create tabix index
bcftools index



Mapping file format: Tab-separated, one line per chromosome — old name in column 1, new name in column 2:
I       1
II      2
III     3
IV      4
V       5
X       6
MtDNA   7
Inputs / Outputs:








Direction
File
Description




Input
*.vcf.gz + .tbi
Source VCF (from input channel or BCFTOOLS_EXTRACT_STRAINS)


Input
chr_mapping.tsv
Chromosome name mapping from LOCAL_GET_CONTIG_INFO

VCF with numeric chromosome names

title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
3. PLINK_RECODE_MS_VCF / PLINK_RECODE_CV_VCF
Source: modules/plink/recode_vcf/main.nf (aliased twice in main.nf)
The same module is imported under two alias names and called in parallel for each strain group:

PLINK_RECODE_MS_VCF — marker SNP set run. Uses per-group ms_maf and ms_ld thresholds from the strainfile. Outputs TO_SIMS.* files used for GRM construction and GWA mapping (Phase 3).
PLINK_RECODE_CV_VCF — causal variant pool run. Uses params.cv_maf (defaults to the group’s ms_maf if not set) and params.cv_ld thresholds. Outputs CV_TO_SIMS.* files used for causal variant selection in Phase 2.

Both runs perform LD pruning and produce PLINK binary filesets through two sequential PLINK commands: first to identify LD-pruned variants, then to create the binary files using only those variants.

Command 1: LD pruning
plink --vcf recoded.vcf.gz \
    --snps-only \
    --biallelic-only \
    --maf ${maf} \
    --set-missing-var-ids @:# \
    --indep-pairwise 50 10 ${ld} \
    --geno \
    --not-chr ${mito_name} \
    --allow-extra-chr
This command computes pairwise LD and produces plink.prune.in (variant IDs to retain) and plink.prune.out (variant IDs to remove).



Flag
Purpose
Docs




--snps-only
Exclude indels, retain only SNPs
plink filter


--biallelic-only
Exclude multiallelic sites
plink filter


--maf ${maf}
Remove variants below the minor allele frequency threshold
plink filter


--set-missing-var-ids @:#
Assign IDs to variants missing an rsID, using CHROM:POS format
plink data


--indep-pairwise 50 10 ${ld}
Sliding-window LD pruning: window=50 variants, step=10, r2 threshold from ld input (per-group ms_ld for the MS run; cv_ld for the CV run)
plink LD


--geno
Exclude variants with missing genotype rates above the default threshold (0.1)
plink filter


--not-chr ${mito_name}
Exclude the mitochondrial chromosome
plink filter


--allow-extra-chr
Permit non-standard chromosome codes
plink filter





Command 2: Binary fileset creation
plink --vcf recoded.vcf.gz \
    --make-bed \
    --snps-only \
    --biallelic-only \
    --maf ${maf} \
    --set-missing-var-ids @:# \
    --extract plink.prune.in \
    --geno \
    --recode \
    --out TO_SIMS \
    --allow-extra-chr
Re-applies the same filtering flags for consistency and additionally:



Flag
Purpose
Docs




--make-bed
Create PLINK binary fileset (.bed, .bim, .fam)
plink data


--extract plink.prune.in
Retain only variants that passed LD pruning
plink data


--recode
Also produce text-format .ped/.map files
plink data


--out TO_SIMS
Set output file prefix
—





Post-processing: markers.txt
awk -F":" '$1=$1' OFS="\t" plink.prune.in | \
    sort -k1,1d -k2,2n > markers.txt
Converts the CHROM:POS variant IDs from plink.prune.in into a tab-separated CHROM  POS file, sorted by chromosome then position. This marker list is used by BCFTOOLS_CREATE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX to subset the VCF.


Inputs / Outputs








Direction
File
Description




Input
recoded.vcf.gz + .tbi
Chromosome-renamed, strain-subset VCF


Input
mito_name (val)
Numeric mitochondrial chromosome ID (looked up from LOCAL_GET_CONTIG_INFO mapping using the --mito_name parameter; e.g., 7 for MtDNA)


Input
maf (val)
Minor allele frequency threshold (ms_maf for the MS run; cv_maf or fallback ms_maf for the CV run)


Input
ld (val)
LD R² pruning threshold (ms_ld for the MS run; cv_ld for the CV run)


Output (MS run)
TO_SIMS.bed/.bim/.fam
PLINK binary fileset for GRM construction and GWA mapping (Phase 3)


Output (MS run)
TO_SIMS.map/.ped
PLINK text-format fileset


Output (MS run)
TO_SIMS.nosex/.log
PLINK auxiliary outputs


Output (CV run)
CV_TO_SIMS.bed/.bim/.fam
PLINK binary fileset for causal variant selection (Phase 2)


Output (CV run)
CV_TO_SIMS.map/.ped
PLINK text-format fileset


Output (CV run)
CV_TO_SIMS.nosex/.log
PLINK auxiliary outputs


Output (MS run)
recoded.vcf.gz + .tbi
Copy of the input VCF (passed through for downstream use)


Output (MS run)
markers.txt
Tab-separated list of retained markers (CHROM, POS)









Note



The TO_SIMS.bim file (MS run output) is consumed by DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_MARKER_SET, which writes the LD-pruned marker list to {db}/markers/marker_sets/{marker_set_id}_markers.parquet. marker_set_id is the 20-character SHA-256 hash (v=2) computed from (population, maf, species, vcf_release_id, ms_ld) by generate_marker_set_id() in R/database.R. DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_MARKER_SET also requires n_indep_tests (number of independent tests from EIGEN analysis) before it can run; it stores this in a per-population metadata file under marker_set_metadata/. n_indep_tests is computed by R_FIND_GENOTYPE_MATRIX_EIGEN using full eigendecomposition of the genotype correlation matrix per chromosome (Li & Ji 2005): for each eigenvalue λ, contribute 1 if λ > 1, else the fractional part of λ. Chromosome counts are summed by LOCAL_COMPILE_EIGENS.
The CV_TO_SIMS.* binary (CV run output) is not written to the database. It is passed to Phase 2 (PYTHON_SIMULATE_EFFECTS_GLOBAL) for causal variant selection, then forwarded to GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES as the --bfile source. Only the MS binary drives GWA mapping.

title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
4. BCFTOOLS_CREATE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX
Source: modules/bcftools/create_genotype_matrix/main.nf
Creates a numeric genotype matrix (markers × strains) from the VCF, restricted to the LD-pruned marker set. Genotypes are encoded numerically for use in eigenvalue decomposition and downstream R analysis.
Commands:
# List and sort sample names
bcftools query -l ${vcf} | sort > sorted_samples.txt

# Extract genotypes for pruned markers, recode to numeric
bcftools view -v snps -S sorted_samples.txt -R ${markers} ${vcf} | \
    bcftools query --print-header -f '%CHROM\t%POS\t%REF\t%ALT[\t%GT]\n' | \
    sed 's/[[# 0-9]*]//g' | \   # clean header annotations
    sed 's/:GT//g' | \           # remove :GT suffix from sample names
    sed 's/0|0/-1/g' | \         # REF/REF homozygous → -1
    sed 's/1|1/1/g' | \          # ALT/ALT homozygous →  1
    sed 's/0|1/NA/g' | \         # heterozygous → NA
    sed 's/1|0/NA/g' | \         # heterozygous → NA
    sed 's/.|./NA/g' | \         # missing → NA
    sed 's/0\/0/-1/g' | \        # (unphased) REF/REF → -1
    sed 's/1\/1/1/g' | \         # (unphased) ALT/ALT →  1
    sed 's/0\/1/NA/g' | \        # (unphased) het → NA
    sed 's/1\/0/NA/g' | \        # (unphased) het → NA
    sed 's/.\/./NA/g' \          # (unphased) missing → NA
    > ${group}_${maf}_Genotype_Matrix.tsv



Command
Purpose
Docs




bcftools query -l
List sample names from the VCF header
bcftools query


bcftools view -v snps -S ... -R ...
Subset to SNPs, reorder samples, restrict to marker regions
bcftools view


bcftools query -f
Extract fields in a custom tab-separated format
bcftools query




Genotype encoding



Genotype
Encoding
Meaning




0\|0 or 0/0
-1
Homozygous reference


1\|1 or 1/1
1
Homozygous alternate


0\|1, 1\|0, 0/1, 1/0
NA
Heterozygous


.\|., ./.
NA
Missing



Design note: The encoding assumes inbred diploid organisms. C. elegans strains are expected to be homozygous, so heterozygous calls are treated as missing data (NA) rather than given an intermediate value.


Inputs / Outputs








Direction
File
Description




Input
recoded.vcf.gz + .tbi
Chromosome-renamed, strain-subset VCF


Input
markers.txt
LD-pruned marker positions from PLINK_RECODE_MS_VCF


Output
${group}_${maf}_Genotype_Matrix.tsv
Tab-separated genotype matrix: rows are markers (CHROM, POS, REF, ALT), columns are strains









Note



This TSV is consumed by two downstream paths: the legacy path (R_GET_GCTA_INTERVALS, R_ASSESS_SIMS) uses it for variance explained estimation; the DB path (DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX) pivots it to long format and stores it in {db}/markers/genotypes/{marker_set_id}_genotypes.parquet for future offline analysis. marker_set_id is the 20-character SHA-256 hash of the population and MAF threshold, computed by generate_marker_set_id() in R/database.R.

title: Development
text:
Publishing to GitHub Pages
The documentation site can be published as a GitHub Pages site for the repository. There are two approaches depending on whether you want to commit the rendered HTML or build it in CI.

Publish from a Branch (No CI Required)
This approach uses the quarto publish command to push rendered HTML to a dedicated gh-pages branch. No CI configuration is needed.
One-time setup:

Run quarto publish from the project root, targeting GitHub Pages:
quarto publish gh-pages docs/
This renders the site, creates or updates a gh-pages branch containing only the rendered output, and pushes it to the remote.
In the GitHub repository settings, go to Settings > Pages and configure:

Source: Deploy from a branch
Branch: gh-pages, folder / (root)


The site will be available at https://andersenlab.github.io/nemascan-sims-nf/.
Branch note: quarto publish gh-pages works from any branch. It renders the .qmd files in your current working tree and pushes the output to the gh-pages branch, regardless of which branch you are on. This means you can preview-publish docs from a feature branch (e.g. doc-updates) before merging to main.
To update the published site after changing .qmd files:
quarto publish gh-pages docs/
This is a manual step — the site is not automatically rebuilt on push.

title: Development
text:
Testing Failure Handling Locally
To exercise the failure → manifest → replay loop on -profile test,docker without an HPC allocation, follow the recipe below. This was the protocol used to validate Step 15 V1/V2 on fix/error-handling-pr3 (2026-05-05). For the full operator-facing description of the model see Replay & Error Handling.
1. Inject a deterministic failure. Open modules/gcta/simulate_phenotypes/main.nf and add a conditional exit 1 after the source ${projectDir}/bin/failure_trap.sh line so the EXIT trap is registered before the failure fires:
# In modules/gcta/simulate_phenotypes/main.nf, just after the
# `source ${projectDir}/bin/failure_trap.sh` line:
if [ "${rep}" = "2" ] && [ "${nqtl}" = "5" ]; then exit 1; fi
2. Run the first invocation with --reps 4 so the cell survives a single dropped rep:
nextflow run main.nf -profile test,docker \
    --reps 4 \
    --output_dir ./results/step15-e2e/run






Important



--output_dir must live inside the project tree under Docker — Nextflow does not auto-bind arbitrary paths like /tmp/..., and DB writes will be silently lost on container exit.


3. Inspect the artifacts:
# Per-failure JSON record
ls ./results/replay-test/run/.failures/

# Manifest — one row for the failed (rep=2, nqtl=5) slot
cat ./results/replay-test/run/replay.tsv

# DB has 3 of 4 reps for the failed cell
ls ./results/replay-test/run/db/traits/*.parquet | wc -l
The pipeline exits 0 even though a rep failed.
4. Revert the injection before replaying:
git checkout -- modules/gcta/simulate_phenotypes/main.nf
5. Run the replay invocation:
nextflow run main.nf -profile test,docker,replay \
    -resume \
    --reps 4 \
    --output_dir ./results/replay-test/run \
    --replay ./results/replay-test/run/replay.tsv
6. Verify the cache pattern with nextflow log:
nextflow log -f name,status,hash $(nextflow log -q | tail -1)
The replayed slot’s GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES and downstream tasks should report COMPLETED; prep-phase tasks should report CACHED. Sibling reps in the same cell are filtered out before fanout and will not appear in the log at all. VALIDATE_REPLICATION_COMPLETE exits 0 and the DB contains the full 4 reps.

title: Development
text:
Cleaning Up Nextflow Run Artifacts
After running the pipeline locally, Nextflow leaves behind several directories and log files that are git-ignored but consume disk space:







Path
Contents




.nextflow/
Nextflow cache (task hashes, metadata)


.nextflow.log*
Run logs (.nextflow.log, .nextflow.log.1, …)


work/
Task working directories (can be very large)


Analysis_Results-*
Date-stamped output directories (includes pipeline-info/ with execution reports and timelines)



To remove them:
rm -rf .nextflow .nextflow.log* work Analysis_Results-*






Tip



If you plan to resume a run with nextflow run … -resume, keep work/ and .nextflow/ intact — Nextflow uses them to skip completed tasks.

title: Development
text:
Work Directory Size Audit
After a pipeline run on Rockfish, collect per-task disk metrics with scripts/scan_workdir.sh, then render notebooks/summarize_workdir.qmd to visualize results.
Step 1 — collect:
bash scripts/scan_workdir.sh <work_dir> <output_tsv> <run_name> [--nf_dir PATH] [--processes LIST] [--parallel N]
Run nextflow log from your pipeline directory to get the run name. The script prints progress every 30 s and writes a TSV with one row per task.






Warning



du on large work directories can take 30+ minutes and consume significant CPU. Do not run on a login node. Either start an interactive job first or submit as a batch script:
# Interactive job (Rockfish)
interact -a eande106 -c 4 -t 2:00:00

# Or submit as a batch job
sbatch --account=eande106 --time=2:00:00 --cpus-per-task=8 \
  --wrap="bash scripts/scan_workdir.sh <work_dir> <output_tsv> <run_name> --parallel 8"


Step 2 — visualize:
quarto render notebooks/summarize_workdir.qmd \
  -P tsv_in:<output_tsv> \
  -P out_dir:<results_dir>
Produces a self-contained HTML report and saves plots/summary TSVs to out_dir.

title: Replay & Error Handling
text:
Failed post-trait simulation fanout replicates no longer terminate the pipeline. A task that exhausts its retries is dropped from its downstream channel; the surviving reps complete to the database; and workflow.onComplete emits a replay.tsv listing the dropped slots. A second invocation with -profile replay --replay <tsv> -resume recovers them. The target replication is achieved across two runs: the first writes the reps that succeed (N − k of N), and the replay fills in the remaining k.







Component
Role




errorStrategy = { workflow.stubRun ? 'retry' : (task.attempt <= task.maxRetries ? 'retry' : 'ignore') }
Post-fanout processes drop on retry exhaustion. Stub runs preserve retry semantics. (conf/rockfish.config)


bin/failure_trap.sh
Bash EXIT trap installed by each post-fanout process. Writes ${output_dir}/.failures/<key>.json on non-zero exit.


workflow.onComplete (main.nf)
Aggregates the .failures/ records and writes ${output_dir}/replay.tsv.


--replay filter (main.nf:462, 487)
Restricts fanout to the 6-tuple (species, group, nqtl, effect, h2, rep) slots listed in the manifest.


DB_CLEAN_REPLAY_SLOTS
Deletes the failed slots’ partial DB rows (12 files per slot) before the replay rewrites them.


VALIDATE_REPLICATION_COMPLETE
Emits a warning if any parameter combination is short. Short replication is non-fatal — the validator exits 0.



Prep-phase processes (bcftools_*, plink_recode_*, r_find_genotype_matrix_eigen) stay on terminate. A prep-phase failure is cone-level (the whole (group, maf) subtree fails), not rep-level, so per-rep replay is meaningless upstream of fanout — and the trap cannot write a valid manifest record there. See nemascan-sims-nf-notes/Development/error-handling/planning/halt-with-replay-manifest.md for the design rationale.

title: Replay & Error Handling
text:
First run
Standard invocation — no replay-specific flags:
nextflow run main.nf -profile rockfish \
    --strainfile strains.tsv \
    --vcf 20231213 \
    --output_dir ${OUTPUT}
The pipeline runs to completion regardless of per-rep failures. Exit status is 0 under errorStrategy = 'ignore', even when reps were dropped (short replication is a warning, not an error). Inspect the run:
# Per-failure JSON records (one per failed task, written by the EXIT trap)
ls ${OUTPUT}/.failures/

# Aggregated manifest (one data row per failed slot)
cat ${OUTPUT}/replay.tsv

# Columns
head -1 ${OUTPUT}/replay.tsv | tr '\t' '\n'
# session, species, group, maf, nqtl, effect, h2, rep, mode, type, attempt, exit
If replay.tsv does not exist or has only a header row, every rep succeeded and no replay is needed.






WarningReplay run



Canonical recovery command:
nextflow run main.nf -profile rockfish,replay \
    -resume \
    --output_dir ${OUTPUT} \
    --replay ${OUTPUT}/replay.tsv
The replay profile is defined at nextflow.config:189–192; it adds no resource overrides today and exists so operators have a single canonical spelling.
What the replay run does:

Filters fanout to the 6-tuple (species, group, nqtl, effect, h2,   rep) slots listed in the manifest (main.nf:462).
Filters sibling reps out of the channel before fanout. Sibling reps in the same parameter combination will not appear as CACHED in the log — they do not appear at all. The replay-of-replay invocation (running the same manifest a second time with no replay-side failure) is the cache-hit oracle: all tasks report CACHED.
Cleans the partial DB rows for each replayed slot before rewriting them. DB_CLEAN_REPLAY_SLOTS deletes 12 files per slot:







Count
Path pattern




1
traits/{trait_id}.parquet


1
traits/causal_variants/{trait_id}_causal.parquet


1
traits/causal_genotypes/{trait_id}_causal_geno.parquet


1
traits/phenotypes/{trait_id}_phenotype.parquet


8
mappings/population={group}/mapping_id={mid}/{data,meta}.parquet for each of the 4 mode × pca combos



Sibling reps, other parameter combinations, marker_set_metadata/, and markers/ are not touched.
Runs the replayed slot with a fresh seed. Causal-variant selection in bin/create_causal_vars.py and bin/Create_Causal_QTLs.R is intentionally non-deterministic (np.random.default_rng() is unseeded; the R script has no set.seed() call). The replayed rep is therefore drawn from a different realization than the failed attempt.


Manage replay.tsv between runs
After a successful replay, the input replay.tsv is not archived, emptied, or moved. The file persists on disk exactly as the first run wrote it. Before any subsequent -resume invocation on the same output_dir, archive or delete it:
mv ${OUTPUT}/replay.tsv ${OUTPUT}/replay.tsv.applied.$(date +%F)
Re-running with the same stale manifest is harmful: DB_CLEAN_REPLAY_SLOTS re-deletes the files the previous replay just rewrote, and Nextflow’s work-dir cache then serves the downstream producers as CACHED — leaving the DB with missing files. Treat replay.tsv as a one-shot input.

Replay only within the scratch retention window
The replay relies on Nextflow’s -resume cache hitting the work directories of the first run’s successful tasks. If the work dir has been evicted from scratch, -resume cannot serve the prep-phase tasks from cache; the prep phase re-runs, the sibling reps within the replayed slot’s parameter combination re-run under fresh seeds (because causal-variant selection is non-deterministic), and the DB ends up with cross-rep RNG inconsistency within that parameter combination.
If the work dir is gone, do not use --replay. Delete ${OUTPUT}/db and rerun the full pipeline.


Statistical-validity caveat for replayed reps
Reps recovered via replay are conditioned on “did not trigger the failure mode that dropped them.” If the failure mode correlates with a biologically-meaningful property of the simulation — for example, a low MAF causing low Vp that pushes a rep into the GCTA_MAKE_GRM upscaling failure path — replayed reps are not exchangeable with first-run reps for the same parameter combination.
Before running --replay, inspect replay.tsv and confirm the failures are not concentrated within a specific parameter combination of the replication regime. If they are, the failure mode is correlated with that combination’s biology and replaying will bias its per-rep statistics. See the same callout in Database Structure for the per-table implication.
